heroOverview: Postherpetic neuralgia (PHN) is persistent neuropathic pain lasting more than 90 days after the onset of herpes zoster (shingles), caused by damage to peripheral sensory nerves inflicted by the reactivating varicella-zoster virus. Approximately 10-18% of all shingles episodes progress to PHN, equating to roughly 1 million new cases in the United States each year. The pain — typically burning, stabbing, or allodynic — can last months to years and is rated among the most severe and disabling of neuropathic pain conditions. Vaccination with the recombinant zoster vaccine (Shingrix) reduces shingles incidence by 97% and the risk of PHN among those who develop shingles by approximately 90%. Pharmacological treatment with gabapentinoids, tricyclic antidepressants, and topical lidocaine or capsaicin patch achieves clinically meaningful relief in 40-60% of patients.

keyStats.prevalence: 10-18% of shingles episodes progress to PHN; approximately 1 million cases per year in the United States (CDC 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Risk increases steeply with age: PHN develops in under 5% of those under 60 but in 20-30% of those over 70; women are affected slightly more often than men
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at onset 65-70 years; rare under age 50; average age of the US shingles patient who develops PHN is 70-75
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 3.4 million shingles cases in the US per year; global shingles incidence approximately 3-5 per 1,000 person-years, rising with population aging

definition: Postherpetic neuralgia (PHN; ICD-10: B02.29 — herpes zoster with other nervous system involvement, or B02.2 — herpes zoster with other complications including neuralgia) is the most common serious complication of herpes zoster, defined as pain persisting in the affected dermatome for 90 or more days after rash onset. It results from virus-induced damage to primary afferent neurons, dorsal root ganglion cells, and spinal cord dorsal horn circuits. Structural changes include axon degeneration of A-beta, A-delta, and C fibers, with subsequent ectopic discharge from surviving damaged axons and central sensitization at the dorsal horn. The condition manifests as a combination of ongoing spontaneous pain (burning, aching, or stabbing) and stimulus-evoked pain, including allodynia — pain from normally non-painful stimuli such as clothing contact — and hyperalgesia. The thoracic dermatomes (T3-T12) account for over 50% of cases; the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve (herpes zoster ophthalmicus) is the second most common site and carries risk of corneal involvement and vision loss. PHN is managed principally by pain medicine specialists, with dermatologists, neurologists, and ophthalmologists involved for specific complications. The condition represents the single most common indication for chronic neuropathic pain treatment in older adults.

typesClassification.description: Characterized by marked allodynia to light touch with relatively preserved sensory thresholds. Patients cannot tolerate clothing, bedsheets, or air movement over the affected area. Large-fiber A-beta afferents are partially preserved and carry allodynic signals via central sensitization mechanisms. Topical treatments (lidocaine patch, capsaicin 8% patch) are particularly effective in this subtype.

Characterized by dense sensory loss in the affected dermatome with ongoing spontaneous burning pain from deafferented central pathways. Severe axon degeneration is the underlying mechanism. Responds poorly to topical therapy due to absent peripheral targets; systemic pharmacotherapy (gabapentinoids, TCAs, strong opioids) is required.

Affects the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve following herpes zoster ophthalmicus. Associated with corneal scarring, uveitis, and risk of vision loss in addition to the characteristic facial neuropathic pain. Ophthalmological co-management is essential. Overall, approximately 10-25% of zoster cases involve the trigeminal distribution.

primarySymptoms: Persistent burning pain in the distribution of the previously affected dermatome, often described as a continuous background ache with superimposed sharp, stabbing, or shooting exacerbations.

Allodynia — severe pain from normally innocuous skin contact, including clothing, light touch, and temperature changes — reported by 70-90% of PHN patients and often the most functionally disabling feature.
Hyperalgesia — exaggerated pain response to mildly painful stimuli such as pinprick in the affected dermatome.
Spontaneous dysesthesias — tingling, electric, crawling, or itching sensations in the affected skin area, unrelated to external stimulation.
Constant deep aching pain in muscles and bones underlying the affected dermatome, particularly in thoracic and lumbar distributions.
Paroxysmal lancinating pain — sudden, intense electrical jolts lasting seconds that interrupt baseline pain and are unpredictable in timing.
Insomnia from pain-related sleep disturbance, particularly from tactile allodynia that prevents comfortable positioning in bed.
Cutaneous changes in the affected dermatome including scarring, hyperpigmentation, altered sweating, and areas of altered or lost sensation.
Psychological distress — depression and anxiety — in up to 50% of patients with severe PHN, reinforced by the chronic, treatment-refractory nature of the pain.
earlyWarningSigns: Dermatomal pain or paresthesias preceding the herpes zoster rash by 1-5 days (prodromal phase) — early antiviral treatment at this stage reduces PHN risk.
Severe acute pain during the active shingles episode — pain intensity during the acute phase is one of the strongest predictors of PHN development.
Herpes zoster ophthalmicus with eye involvement — requires urgent ophthalmological assessment to prevent corneal and vision complications.
Rash involvement of the ophthalmic nerve division (forehead, scalp, tip of nose — Hutchinson sign) — strongly predicts intraocular involvement.
Persistence of significant dermal pain beyond 30 days after rash onset — this half-way diagnostic window identifies patients who should begin preventive neuropathic pharmacotherapy.
emergencySigns: Visual changes, eye redness, or photophobia in herpes zoster ophthalmicus — requires same-day ophthalmological review to exclude corneal ulceration, uveitis, or acute angle-closure glaucoma.
Herpes zoster affecting the ear with facial nerve palsy, sensorineural hearing loss, and vesicles in the ear canal (Ramsay Hunt syndrome) — requires urgent ENT assessment and corticosteroids.
Meningoencephalitis signs — fever, neck stiffness, altered consciousness in a patient with active herpes zoster — rare but requires emergency neurology assessment and intravenous acyclovir.
Disseminated herpes zoster (spread beyond three dermatomes) in an immunocompromised patient — signals systemic VZV viremia requiring intravenous antiviral therapy.

causes.cause: Peripheral nerve damage by varicella-zoster virus reactivation
causes.description: During primary varicella infection, VZV establishes latency in dorsal root and cranial nerve ganglia. Upon reactivation, the virus travels antidromically along sensory nerve fibers, causing inflammation, demyelination, and axon degeneration in the affected dermatome. The extent of axon loss in the acute phase is the principal determinant of PHN development and severity.

causes.cause: Ectopic discharge from damaged primary afferent neurons
causes.description: Injured sensory neurons surviving the VZV-mediated assault develop abnormal spontaneous discharge from upregulated sodium channels and altered transduction proteins. This peripheral ectopic activity drives the continuous background pain and lancinating pain characteristic of PHN.

causes.cause: Central sensitization in the spinal cord dorsal horn
causes.description: Prolonged abnormal peripheral input from damaged afferents sensitizes dorsal horn second-order neurons via NMDA receptor activation. This central sensitization amplifies all incoming sensory signals, producing allodynia (A-beta fiber signaling misinterpreted as pain) and hyperalgesia, and may persist even after peripheral activity subsides.

causes.cause: Deafferentation of spinal cord circuits from profound nerve loss
causes.description: In severe cases, extensive peripheral nerve degeneration creates a deafferentation state in which the dorsal horn loses normal inhibitory control, generating spontaneous pain from centrally dysregulated circuits. This subtype is the most treatment-refractory form of PHN.

Aging reduces VZV-specific cell-mediated immunity and the capacity for peripheral nerve regeneration. Older patients have more extensive nerve damage during acute zoster, more severe acute pain, and substantially higher PHN conversion rates. The risk of PHN doubles approximately every decade after age 60.

riskFactors.description: The strongest risk factor for PHN: 20-30% of patients over 70 develop PHN after zoster versus under 5% of those under 60. Age-related immunosenescence reduces viral control and nerve repair capacity.

riskFactors.factor: Severe acute herpes zoster pain (≥5/10 at rash onset)
riskFactors.description: Acute pain intensity predicts the degree of nerve damage; patients with severe acute pain are 2-3x more likely to develop PHN and to have more severe, prolonged PHN.
Presence of dermatomal pain in the days before the rash indicates earlier and more extensive ganglionic inflammation, associated with higher PHN rates in multiple prospective studies.
Extensive vesicular eruption over a large dermatome area correlates with greater viral load and more widespread nerve damage, increasing both PHN incidence and severity.

Trigeminal PHN, particularly the ophthalmic division, is among the most severe and persistent PHN subtypes; facial and periocular allodynia are functionally devastating and more treatment-refractory than thoracic PHN.

HIV infection, malignancy, chemotherapy, transplant immunosuppression, and high-dose corticosteroids increase zoster reactivation risk by 10-100× and increase the probability of PHN and disseminated disease.
Shingrix (recombinant zoster vaccine) reduces shingles incidence by 97% and PHN risk among those who develop shingles by approximately 90%. Unvaccinated adults over 50 carry preventable PHN risk.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 60: approximately 1 million US shingles cases per year produce 100,000-180,000 PHN cases; the vast majority in patients over 60.
Immunocompromised patients: HIV, hematological malignancy, transplant recipients, and patients on long-term immunosuppressive therapy have 10-100× higher zoster incidence, with higher PHN rates.

Women: slightly higher shingles incidence and PHN rates compared with age-matched men in most epidemiological datasets; hormonal factors and greater central sensitization susceptibility have been proposed.
Rural and medically underserved populations: lower vaccination uptake and delayed antiviral treatment during acute zoster are associated with higher PHN rates in low-income and rural communities.
diagnosisOverview: PHN is a clinical diagnosis based on the characteristic history and examination findings. The core diagnostic criteria are: (1) history of herpes zoster with the characteristic dermatomal vesicular rash, (2) pain in the distribution of the affected dermatome, and (3) duration of pain exceeding 90 days from rash onset. Examination reveals one or more of: allodynia to light touch, hyperalgesia to pinprick, altered temperature sensation, or sensory loss in the affected area. The combination of sensory loss and ongoing pain (paradoxical anesthesia dolorosa) is particularly indicative. No laboratory testing is required in typical presentations. Skin-blot VZV PCR or direct fluorescent antibody testing of acute lesion scrapings confirm the zoster diagnosis if the history is unclear. Quantitative sensory testing characterizes the sensory phenotype — allodynic versus deafferentation type — and guides treatment selection (topical therapy for allodynic; systemic for deafferentation). In the thoracic distribution, PHN must be distinguished from musculoskeletal chest wall pain, pleuritis, and intercostal neuralgia from other causes. In the trigeminal distribution, trigeminal neuralgia (paroxysmal lancinating pain without sensory loss) is the principal differential. Imaging is not routinely indicated unless CNS involvement is suspected.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination of dermatome sensory function
diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterizes the PHN subtype — allodynic (light touch hypersensitivity) versus deafferentation (sensory loss with ongoing pain) — which guides treatment selection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A structured sensory examination using a light cotton wisp, a 28-gauge pin, and temperature stimuli (cold ice, warm object) applied to the affected and mirror dermatomes. Takes 10-15 minutes. No equipment required beyond standard clinical tools.

diagnosticTests.test: VZV PCR or direct fluorescent antibody testing of skin lesion
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms herpes zoster etiology when the acute rash history is atypical, resolved, or in doubt
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab of the base of a vesicular lesion (or crust if lesion is healing) sent for PCR analysis. Results in 1-3 days. Relevant only in the acute phase; positive results in established PHN are not required for diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies pain intensity and neuropathic character; tracks treatment response longitudinally
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Self-administered questionnaires taking 5-10 minutes. The Neuropathic Pain Scale (NPS) captures dimensions of burning, sensitivity, and unpleasantness relevant to PHN. DN4 distinguishes neuropathic from nociceptive pain.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Formally measures warm detection threshold, cold pain threshold, and mechanical pain sensitivity to characterize the PHN sensory phenotype and predict treatment response to topical agents
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standardized thermal and mechanical stimuli applied to the affected dermatome and mirror side. Takes 30-60 minutes in a specialist center. Preserved thermal detection suggests allodynic subtype; loss indicates deafferentation type.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies depression and anxiety, prevalent comorbidities in PHN that worsen functional outcomes and require concurrent treatment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard validated questionnaires, 5-10 minutes. Depression is present in 20-50% of PHN patients and significantly amplifies pain perception and reduces treatment response.

treatmentOverview: Management follows a stepwise pharmacological approach guided by EFNS/IASP and American Pain Society guidelines. First-line treatments are: tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline or nortriptyline 10-75 mg at night), gabapentin (300-1,200 mg three times daily), pregabalin (75-300 mg twice daily), and the topical 5% lidocaine patch — applied for up to 12 hours per day over the painful area. Each of these first-line agents achieves at least 50% pain relief (NNT 3-7) in well-conducted RCTs. Second-line agents include the capsaicin 8% patch (applied in clinic for 60 minutes, repeated every 3 months), which desensitizes TRPV1 receptors in surviving C fibers, and duloxetine 60 mg daily for the combined pain and mood benefit. Strong opioids (oxycodone, morphine, or tramadol) are third-line due to modest efficacy (NNT 3-5), significant side-effect burden, and long-term dependency risk; the CDC 2022 guidelines recommend reserving them for PHN refractory to first-and second-line agents. In the allodynic subtype, topical lidocaine can be applied as monotherapy or combined with oral agents. Preventive vaccination with Shingrix (two-dose series) is the most effective single intervention: among vaccinated adults over 50, shingles incidence is reduced by 97% and PHN risk by approximately 90% (Lal et al., NEJM 2015, PMID 26091034). Acute antiviral therapy (valacyclovir 1 g three times daily for 7 days, started within 72 hours of rash onset) reduces PHN incidence by approximately 30-50%.

medicalTreatments.name: Gabapentin (300-1,200 mg three times daily) or pregabalin (75-300 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line systemic pharmacotherapy for PHN. Alpha-2-delta calcium channel ligands reduce ectopic nerve firing and dorsal horn sensitization. Titrate over 1-2 weeks to target dose to minimize dizziness and sedation. Dose reduction required in renal impairment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NNT of 4-7 for ≥50% pain relief; response in 40-60% of patients; superiority to placebo confirmed in multiple RCTs (Rice et al., PAIN 2001, PMID 11163993)

medicalTreatments.name: Tricyclic antidepressants — nortriptyline or amitriptyline (10-75 mg at night)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line oral therapy for PHN. Block norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake and sodium channels, enhancing descending pain inhibition. Nortriptyline preferred in older adults for lower anticholinergic burden. Start at 10-25 mg at night and titrate weekly.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NNT of 3-5 for ≥50% pain relief in PHN RCTs; equally effective to gabapentinoids with faster onset at low doses

medicalTreatments.name: Topical 5% lidocaine patch (applied up to 12 hours/day to painful area)
medicalTreatments.description: Stabilizes sodium channels in hypersensitive peripheral afferents without systemic absorption. Particularly effective in allodynic PHN where skin sensation is relatively preserved. Up to three patches can be applied simultaneously. Minimal systemic side-effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NNT of 4-5 in PHN with allodynia; comparable efficacy to oral pregabalin in RCTs with far fewer systemic adverse effects in elderly patients

medicalTreatments.name: Capsaicin 8% patch (QUTENZA) applied in clinic every 3 months
medicalTreatments.description: High-concentration capsaicin desensitizes TRPV1 receptors and causes functional defunctionalization of C fibers in the treated skin. Applied under supervision with pretreatment topical anesthetic for 60 minutes on the affected dermatome; provides pain relief lasting 8-12 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NNT of 6 for ≥50% pain relief; 30-40% of patients respond; provides sustained benefit for 3 months per application in RCTs

medicalTreatments.name: Duloxetine (60-120 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: SNRI providing dual benefit for PHN pain and comorbid depression. Evidence for PHN is less robust than for diabetic neuropathy but supported by mechanistic rationale; guidelines include it as second-line for PHN with significant mood disorder component.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30-50% reduction in pain scores in neuropathic pain trials; particularly beneficial when depression or anxiety is concurrent

medicalTreatments.name: Opioids — oxycodone or tramadol (titrated, third-line)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for PHN refractory to first- and second-line agents. Provide analgesia independent of the neuropathic mechanism via mu-opioid receptors. Associated with constipation, falls risk, and cognitive impairment in elderly patients. Tramadol has added serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibition.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NNT of 3-5 for ≥50% pain relief; significant adverse effect burden, particularly in adults over 70; risk of opioid use disorder limits long-term use

surgicalOptions.name: Spinal cord stimulation (SCS) for refractory PHN
surgicalOptions.description: Epidural electrode array at the dorsal thoracic or cervical spinal cord level, used for PHN unresponsive to all pharmacological strategies. Dorsal root ganglion stimulation (DRGS) is increasingly preferred for precise dermatomal targeting. Requires a trial period before permanent implantation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 50-70% of carefully selected refractory PHN patients achieve ≥50% pain relief with SCS or DRGS; high-frequency SCS modes improve outcomes in some series

alternativeTreatments.name: Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS)
alternativeTreatments.description: Non-invasive surface electrical stimulation applied to the affected area. Modest evidence for short-term pain reduction in PHN as an adjunct to pharmacotherapy; safe, inexpensive, and self-administered. Cochrane review (Handwerker 2020) found limited but consistent benefit.

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-level laser therapy (LLLT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Photobiomodulation applied to the affected dermatome; two small RCTs found significant pain reduction in thoracic PHN compared with sham; mechanism may involve enhanced nerve repair and reduced neuroinflammation.

whySeeSpecialist: A pain medicine specialist should be involved when PHN pain persists beyond 3 months without adequate relief from first-line agents, when allodynia is severely disabling, or when opioid therapy is being considered. Ophthalmological input is urgent for herpes zoster ophthalmicus. A dermatologist manages persistent skin complications.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a board-certified pain medicine physician (ABPM or neurology with neuropathic pain specialization) familiar with PHN treatment guidelines, topical therapies, and capsaicin patch protocols. Look for a clinic with access to quantitative sensory testing and spinal cord stimulation for severe refractory cases.
hospitalCriteria: Access to a specialist pain clinic with QST capability
Ability to administer capsaicin 8% patch under monitored conditions
Ophthalmology service for herpes zoster ophthalmicus
ENT service for Ramsay Hunt syndrome
Spinal cord stimulation program for refractory PHN
keyFacilities: Specialist neuropathic pain clinics at academic medical centers
Multidisciplinary pain programs with pharmacological and interventional capability
Dermatology clinics for acute zoster management and referral pathways
Ophthalmology centers for trigeminal PHN and ocular herpes zoster
preventionStrategies: Vaccinate all adults aged 50 and over with the two-dose recombinant zoster vaccine (Shingrix); vaccination reduces shingles incidence by 97% and PHN risk among vaccine breakthrough cases by approximately 90%.

Start antiviral therapy (valacyclovir 1 g three times daily, famciclovir 500 mg three times daily, or acyclovir 800 mg five times daily) within 72 hours of rash onset to reduce nerve damage and PHN incidence by 30-50%.
Initiate aggressive pain management during acute zoster — adequate acute pain control reduces the central sensitization cascade that predisposes to chronic PHN.

Consider short-course corticosteroids (prednisolone 60 mg/day tapering over 3 weeks) in conjunction with antivirals in immunocompetent patients over 50 with severe acute pain or significant quality-of-life impact.

Prioritize vaccination in immunocompromised patients — the recombinant vaccine is safe in most immunocompromised states and is particularly recommended in HIV, haematological malignancy, and transplant recipients.
lifestyleModifications: Protect the affected skin area from clothing contact by wearing loose, soft fabrics (100% cotton or silk) and avoiding synthetic materials that increase allodynia.
Apply cold or cool compresses gently to the affected area for 10-15 minutes several times daily — cold reduces ectopic neural discharge and allodynic responses in many patients.
Maintain a consistent sleep schedule and use topical lidocaine patch in the evening hours to reduce overnight pain disruption.
Practice relaxation techniques (guided imagery, progressive muscle relaxation) to reduce stress-related pain amplification and central sensitization.
Engage in supported social activities and light physical activity to prevent the social isolation, deconditioning, and depression that worsen PHN chronicity.
Attend a structured pain management program if PHN persists beyond 6 months — cognitive behavioral therapy reduces catastrophizing and improves function in chronic neuropathic pain.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory omega-3-rich foods (fatty fish, walnuts, flaxseed) — reduce neuroinflammatory load
Antioxidant-rich foods (berries, leafy greens, colorful vegetables) to support nerve repair and immune function
Adequate B-vitamin intake (B12 in particular) — B12 supports myelin integrity and nerve conduction; deficiency impairs nerve repair after viral injury
Adequate calorie and protein intake to maintain immune function, particularly in elderly or underweight patients
avoid: Excessive alcohol, which impairs sleep, increases central sensitization, and interacts with gabapentinoids and TCAs
High-sugar ultra-processed foods that promote systemic inflammation and worsen neuropathic pain in patients with concurrent diabetes
Foods high in arginine (chocolate, nuts at very high quantities) — theoretical concern for VZV reactivation; evidence is weak but patients often ask about this
Grapefruit and grapefruit juice when on opioids or certain TCAs metabolized by CYP3A4

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise improves sleep, mood, and endogenous pain inhibition in chronic pain conditions including PHN. Walking, swimming, or cycling in a temperature-controlled environment are appropriate. Avoid high-impact activities that provoke jarring vibration to the affected dermatome. Exercise physiologist or physiotherapist supervision is helpful for severely deconditioned elderly patients.
dailyManagement: Take oral medications at consistent times to maintain therapeutic plasma levels — gabapentinoids require regular dosing every 8 hours for consistent effect.
Apply topical lidocaine patch to the most painful skin area for up to 12 hours per day, typically in the evening when allodynia is worst.
Keep a daily pain diary rating intensity, location, sleep quality, and medication side-effects to share with the treating physician for titration decisions.
Use soft, seamless cotton garments and keep bedding lightweight — dedicated texture-sensitive bedding significantly reduces overnight allodynic pain.
Report any vision changes, eye pain, or facial paresis to a clinician immediately — trigeminal PHN complications require urgent specialist review.
Attend scheduled follow-up visits — PHN management requires active dose titration over the first 3-6 months; regular review prevents undertreatment.
prognosis: PHN resolves in approximately 50% of patients within one year, in 70% within two years, and in 80-85% within three years without treatment. However, a persistent, treatment-refractory minority (15-20%) have chronic pain lasting many years. Age is the strongest prognosis predictor: patients under 60 have high spontaneous resolution rates; patients over 70 with severe initial pain have 30-50% rates of persistent pain at 2 years. With appropriate first-line treatment, clinically meaningful pain relief (≥50% reduction) is achieved in 40-60% of patients; complete resolution is achieved in 15-25%. Vaccination with Shingrix, by dramatically reducing both zoster incidence and PHN conversion rates, is transforming the natural history of this condition in vaccinated populations. Untreated or poorly managed PHN carries significant quality-of-life burden: depression, sleep disorder, and social isolation develop in 30-50% of patients with severe PHN, and these psychological complications independently worsen pain and require concurrent treatment.
recoveryTimeline: Spontaneous remission in 50% by 12 months, 70% by 24 months. Active treatment with first-line agents should begin within the first 3 months of PHN onset. Pain reduction is typically apparent within 2-4 weeks of reaching therapeutic doses. Capsaicin 8% patch provides effect within 1-2 weeks, lasting 3 months per application. Spinal cord stimulation benefit develops over weeks to months of programming.
complications: Depression and anxiety in 20-50% of patients with severe PHN — screen with PHQ-9 and treat concurrently; untreated depression worsens pain scores significantly.
Falls and fractures from gabapentinoid-induced dizziness and sedation in elderly patients — start at low doses, titrate slowly, and counsel on fall prevention.
Corneal scarring, uveitis, and permanent vision loss in inadequately treated herpes zoster ophthalmicus — requires prompt ophthalmology co-management.
Social isolation and loss of independence from severe allodynia preventing normal activities, clothing, or human touch — proactive occupational therapy support reduces functional deterioration.
Ramsay Hunt syndrome complications — permanent facial nerve palsy and sensorineural hearing loss in severe cases with delayed treatment.
Opioid dependency from long-term opioid use in treatment-refractory PHN — minimize through adherence to first- and second-line guidelines before resorting to opioids.

supportResources.name: American Pain Foundation — Neuropathic Pain Resources
supportResources.description: IASP fact sheets on neuropathic pain including herpes zoster and PHN patient-facing information.

supportResources.name: CDC Shingles (Herpes Zoster) Vaccination Information
supportResources.description: CDC guidance on Shingrix vaccination schedules, eligibility, and effectiveness for PHN prevention.

supportResources.name: National Shingles Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient-focused advocacy organization providing PHN support resources, treatment guides, and clinician locator.

confusedWithConditions.name: Trigeminal Neuralgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Trigeminal neuralgia produces paroxysmal, intense electric shocks in the face triggered by chewing, talking, or touch, without sensory loss and without a preceding herpes zoster rash. PHN involving the trigeminal nerve follows confirmed herpes zoster, has continuous background pain, and is associated with sensory changes or loss in the affected dermatome.

confusedWithConditions.name: Herpes Simplex Infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Herpes simplex virus causes recurrent oral or genital vesicular eruptions and rarely causes persistent neuropathic pain. Herpes zoster is caused by VZV reactivation from sensory ganglia, follows a dermatome, and causes the characteristic unilateral rash from which PHN arises. Serology or PCR differentiates the two viruses in ambiguous presentations.

confusedWithConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain (other causes)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Neuropathic pain from other causes — diabetic neuropathy, chemotherapy, nerve entrapment — may produce similar burning and allodynic symptoms. PHN is distinguished by the history of dermatomal zoster rash, unilateral distribution, and age over 50 in most cases. Skin changes (scarring, pigmentation) in the affected dermatome provide additional diagnostic clues.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: PHN is one of the most common causes of neuropathic pain in older adults; treatment guidelines for PHN are derived from the broader neuropathic pain evidence base.

relatedConditions.name: Trigeminal Neuralgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are severe neuropathic facial pain syndromes requiring specialist pain management; they share some treatments (gabapentinoids, TCAs) but differ fundamentally in etiology and specific management.

relatedConditions.name: Central Pain Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: The deafferentation subtype of PHN involves central sensitization mechanisms that overlap with central pain syndrome; both require centrally acting pharmacotherapy.

relatedConditions.name: Herpes Simplex Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are caused by herpesviruses; VZV (PHN) and HSV (herpes simplex) share antiviral treatment strategies and may both cause chronic neuropathic complications, though HSV neuralgia is far less common than PHN.

faqs.question: What is postherpetic neuralgia?
faqs.answer: Postherpetic neuralgia is neuropathic pain persisting more than 90 days after a shingles (herpes zoster) rash. It results from damage to sensory nerves caused by the reactivating varicella-zoster virus. The pain is typically burning, allodynic, or stabbing and can last months to years. It develops in 10-18% of shingles episodes.

faqs.question: How long does postherpetic neuralgia last?
faqs.answer: PHN resolves in about 50% of patients within one year, 70% within two years, and 80-85% within three years. However, 15-20% develop persistent pain lasting many years. Older age and severe acute shingles pain strongly predict longer duration. With appropriate treatment, clinically meaningful pain relief is achieved in 40-60% of patients.

faqs.question: What are the best medications for postherpetic neuralgia?
faqs.answer: First-line medications are gabapentin or pregabalin (alpha-2-delta ligands), tricyclic antidepressants (nortriptyline or amitriptyline), and the topical 5% lidocaine patch. The capsaicin 8% patch is a second-line topical option applied in clinic every 3 months. Opioids are third-line due to side-effect burden in older patients.

faqs.question: Can postherpetic neuralgia be prevented?
faqs.answer: Yes. The recombinant zoster vaccine (Shingrix, two-dose series) reduces shingles incidence by 97% in adults over 50 and cuts PHN risk by approximately 90% in those who do develop shingles. Starting antiviral therapy within 72 hours of shingles rash onset also reduces PHN incidence by 30-50%.

faqs.question: Why does shingles cause long-term nerve pain?
faqs.answer: Shingles reactivates varicella-zoster virus from sensory nerve ganglia where it lay dormant since chickenpox. As the virus travels down nerve fibers to the skin, it damages axons and dorsal root ganglion cells. The surviving damaged nerves generate abnormal electrical signals and sensitize spinal cord pain circuits, producing persistent pain after the rash heals.

faqs.question: What is allodynia in postherpetic neuralgia?
faqs.answer: Allodynia is pain triggered by normally non-painful stimuli — clothing, light touch, or mild temperature change on the affected skin. It affects 70-90% of PHN patients and is often the most functionally disabling symptom, preventing comfortable sleep or wearing normal clothing. Topical lidocaine patch is particularly effective for allodynic PHN.

faqs.question: Is postherpetic neuralgia the same as shingles?
faqs.answer: No. Shingles (herpes zoster) is the acute rash-and-pain illness from varicella-zoster virus reactivation, lasting 2-4 weeks. Postherpetic neuralgia is the chronic nerve pain that persists after the rash resolves, by definition lasting more than 90 days. Only 10-18% of shingles cases progress to PHN.

faqs.question: How does the lidocaine patch work for PHN?
faqs.answer: The 5% lidocaine patch blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in hypersensitive nerve endings in the affected skin, reducing ectopic neural discharge without significant systemic absorption. It is applied for up to 12 hours per day directly over the painful area. It is particularly effective in allodynic PHN where skin sensitivity is prominent.

faqs.question: What is the capsaicin 8% patch for PHN?
faqs.answer: The capsaicin 8% (QUTENZA) patch contains high-dose capsaicin that activates and then defunctionalizes TRPV1 receptors in C-fiber nerve endings in the skin. Applied in clinic under controlled conditions for 60 minutes every 3 months, it provides pain relief for 8-12 weeks per treatment in 30-40% of PHN patients.

faqs.question: Does shingles vaccine help postherpetic neuralgia?
faqs.answer: Shingrix vaccination prevents postherpetic neuralgia by preventing the shingles that causes it. Among adults over 50, Shingrix reduces shingles incidence by 97% and PHN risk among vaccine breakthrough cases by approximately 90%. It is recommended for all adults 50 and over, including those with prior shingles.

faqs.question: Can young people get postherpetic neuralgia?
faqs.answer: PHN is rare under age 50, developing in under 5% of shingles cases in that age group. The risk rises steeply with age, reaching 20-30% over age 70. Young immunocompromised patients — HIV, cancer, transplant recipients — are exceptions, as they can develop severe shingles and PHN at any age.

faqs.question: Does gabapentin cure postherpetic neuralgia?
faqs.answer: Gabapentin does not cure PHN but provides clinically meaningful pain relief in 40-60% of patients when titrated to adequate doses. It reduces ectopic nerve firing and central sensitization. Many patients require long-term use, and the drug should be tapered slowly on discontinuation to avoid withdrawal effects.

faqs.question: How is trigeminal PHN treated differently from other PHN?
faqs.answer: Trigeminal PHN (following herpes zoster ophthalmicus) requires the same pharmacological approach as thoracic PHN — gabapentinoids, TCAs, topical agents. Additionally, ophthalmological monitoring is essential to detect corneal complications and uveitis. Corneal anaesthesia from ophthalmic nerve damage can mask serious eye injury.

faqs.question: Is postherpetic neuralgia a disability?
faqs.answer: Severe PHN can constitute a disability when it significantly limits daily activities, sleep, social function, and employment. In older adults, PHN-related functional decline is a common trigger for loss of independence. Formal disability assessments require documentation of pain severity, functional limitations, and treatment history.

faqs.question: What should I do if I think I have shingles?
faqs.answer: Seek medical attention immediately — within 72 hours of rash onset. Starting antiviral therapy (valacyclovir or famciclovir) within this window significantly reduces the risk of developing PHN. Any rash affecting the eye, nose, or forehead requires same-day ophthalmology review due to risk of vision-threatening complications.

symptomKeywords: nerve pain after shingles rash healed
metaDescription: Postherpetic neuralgia: lasting nerve pain after shingles, often burning and severe. Symptoms, causes, prevention and treatment reviewed here.
